7 Multifunctional hybrid nanoparticles for theranostics*

coredata.dc:description: This chapter focuses on the potential of hybrid nanoparticles (NPs) for diagnostics and treatment various disorders, with an emphasis on cancer. The concept of “hybrid NPs” implies to coupling inorganic and organic materials with different physical, chemical, and biological properties to achieve the synergetic effect. It allows an optimization and increasing therapeutic efficacy of original components. The chapter describes the principles and methods of fabrication of hybrid NPs. It also demonstrates how organic and inorganic materials could be combined without sacrificing their biocompatibility and effectiveness. This chapter summarizes and analyzes recent research and progress on novel classes of hybrid NPs. The last part of this chapter is dedicated to future of hybrid NPs and possible clinical applications.

chapter.ce:title:
Multifunctional hybrid nanoparticles for theranostics*
* All authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Life Sciences, National Laboratory Astana, Nazarbayev University, Astana Kazakhstan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Life Sciences, National Laboratory Astana, Nazarbayev University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Essex, Colchester, Essex, United Kingdom
University College London, London, United Kingdom

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This chapter focuses on the potential of hybrid nanoparticles (NPs) for diagnostics and treatment various disorders, with an emphasis on cancer. The concept of “hybrid NPs” implies to coupling inorganic and organic materials with different physical, chemical, and biological properties to achieve the synergetic effect. It allows an optimization and increasing therapeutic efficacy of original components. The chapter describes the principles and methods of fabrication of hybrid NPs. It also demonstrates how organic and inorganic materials could be combined without sacrificing their biocompatibility and effectiveness. This chapter summarizes and analyzes recent research and progress on novel classes of hybrid NPs. The last part of this chapter is dedicated to future of hybrid NPs and possible clinical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The current state of nanotechnology allows fabrication of nanomaterials that can be coupled or loaded with various therapeutic and imaging agents. Such an approach can help to overcome the problems associated with standard methods of drug administration, including, but not limited to, uncontrollable distribution, immune reactions, high toxicity, quick decomposition and clearance from the body, and adverse side effects [1]. The concept of nanoparticles (NPs) application is based on the ability of nanoscale compositions to freely circulate in the blood stream, bypass blood-cell barriers, and ultimately, deliver the loaded therapeutic agents to targeted site.

Up to date, a wide range of NPs have been proposed and developed for biomedical applications. All of them can be subdivided onto two main classes: organic and nonorganic platforms. Organic nanoconstructs, mainly lipid or polymeric materials, possess distinct advantages such as better bioavailability and low toxicity, unlike their nonorganic counterparts. For example, lipid-based NPs have a range of unique properties such as the possibility to be manufactured from natural compounds, low immunologic reactivity, ability to be functionalized with ligands, and the capacity to load agents with various solubility [2–4]. On the other hand, nonorganic materials provide a high capacity for encapsulation of chemical agents. Moreover, they possess superior biophysical properties, including electric and thermal conductivity. For instance, silica-based nanosystems demonstrate a good biocompatibility, large surface area, high tunability, an ability for encapsulation and combination with materials of different origin, and most importantly, excellent optical characteristics [5–7].

Despite proven effectiveness and high potential for multiple biomedical applications, all types of nanoplatforms possess both advantages and weaknesses. To surmount such problems, two or more kinds of NPs could be assembled into one single hybrid nanosystem that would inherit and synergistically combine therapeutic properties of its components. Such a strategy opens an avenue for the effective use of nanoplatforms of different origin and functionality. It allows visualization of affected region, and at the same time, to treat it through the direct physical tissue destruction or delivering active compounds to the cells. The family of new systems combining detection and therapeutic functions were categorized as “theranostics.” This novel class of nanoplatforms provides an opportunity for online monitoring and treatment of various disorders opening a new era for personalized medicine.

Unsurprisingly, in the first turn, the concept “detect-visualize-treat” has attracted an attention of cancer researchers during last decades. It was demonstrated that hybrid NPs can be used for the detection of cancer on molecular and tissue levels [8]. For example, periodic mesoporous silica-coated reduced graphene oxide (GO) and embedded with gold NPs showed a high diagnostic potential for the detection of hydrogen peroxide generated by cancer cell lines [9,10]. In another study, folic acid (FA)–conjugated magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) were synthesized for separation and detection of ovarian cancer cells from a whole blood [11]. Hybrid nanoplatforms might also be efficiently utilized for detection and recognition of tumor site and lymph node metastases. It was demonstrated that the surface of magnetic IONPs can be modified with luteinizing hormone to increase the accuracy of the magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and detection of metastatic cancer cells in lymph nodes, bones, and peripheral organs [12]. Apart from narrow detecting and imaging functionality, iron and gold-based hybrid nanosystems can also be utilized for direct destruction of tumor tissues via thermoablation and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [13–15].

Coating and functionalization of NPs is able of enhancing their detection and therapeutic properties as well. First of all, coating of hybrid NPs can help to avoid their recognition by immune system, and thus, provide longer circulation half-life for incorporated agents helping them to reach the targeted sites [16]. Surface functionalization provides the stabilization of particles against aggregation caused by electric charge and other factors such as interfacial tension. More importantly, the NPs’ surface could be co-equipped by ligands with specific affinity to receptors of targeted cells, including cancer cell lines [17,18]. In this regard, nonorganic platforms offer a wide choice and options for particles surface modification leading to enhanced biodistribution, bioavailability, and cell targeting.

He and Lin emphasized that the hybrid NPs must meet few important criterions, including tunable size, high capacity for agent loading, tailorable surface properties, controllable drug-release pharmacokinetics, enhanced biocompatibility and biodegradability, and an ability of passive tumor targeting [1]. The principle of designing and fabrication of hybrid nanosystems can be roughly subclassified onto two approaches either incorporating active compounds on the surface of hybrid NP or encapsulation it inside the nanocarrier. This principle “Barges vs Tankers” was described by Sailor and Park in their review on hybrid NPs [19]. Both concepts of agents’ incorporation have drawbacks and benefits. For example, attaching of active agent on NPs’ surface would be useful for imaging function or for the delivery of external stimuli-responsive systems. At the same time, “tanker” type of hybrid nanoplatform provides a high loading capacity. Moreover, it is indispensable for delivery of highly reactive or toxic compounds that require a special protection.

It must be also noted that some types of nanosystems can be solely used for dual applications after some modification. As an example, gold nanostructures could be employed for imaging, and at the same time, they might be utilized for heating tissues responding to applied optical or magnetic irradiation [20]. However, in many cases “theranostic” concept dictates coupling two or more platforms, where one component plays a role of therapeutic agent, while the other component is being responsible for the diagnostic function.

The coupling strategies mainly rely on conjugating few groups: amino (NH2), carboxyl (COOH), sulfhydryl (SH), and biotin, resulting in the formation of different types of nanosystems [16]. Other methods of incorporation of active agents into NPs’ structure include electrostatic interaction, entrapping into matrix (polymer, lipid, or protein) and direct incorporation during the polymerization, adsorption to mesoporous structure (for example, to silica) and p-p stacking.

This chapter will discuss principles and methods of synthesis of hybrid NPs of different origin. It summarizes and analyzes recent advances on the applications of novel classes of hybrid nanoplatforms with focus on cancer diagnostics and treatment. The last part of the chapter is dedicated to future of hybrid NPs and their possible clinical applications.

Liposomes are spherical self-assembled entities of a size of 50–150nm. They are fabricated from phospholipids, and consequently, closely resemble biological membranes providing superior biocompatibility. Liposomes are able to encapsulate hydrophilic substances in their core and to bind hydrophobic substances to the lipid bilayer [21]. Liposomes were the first type of therapeutic NPs to obtain clinical approval for use in humans to treat cancer. However, their effectiveness still remains lower than that of the free drugs [22]. Liposomes show a low bioavailability in the body, and therefore, require additional modification to increase circulation time. Moreover, liposomes have weak structural integrity that limits containment of active substances (prone to leakage), and instability during storage period. To overcome these limitations, liposomes have been used in combination with polymer or metal NPs and quantum dots (QDs) [23]. Lipid–polymer hybrid nanoparticles (LPHNs) were developed to overcome limitations faced by liposomes and polymer NPs when used separately.

Polymer NPs usually have strong structural integrity and stability, and they are well known for their ability to release active substances in a controlled manner. However, they are often required to be conjugated with polyethylene glycol (PEG) to increase biocompatibility and circulation time within the organism [19]. Conjugation with PEG can help to reduce hydrophobicity of NPs, thus limiting their interaction with opsonin [24]. Opsonins are proteins found in the blood serum which upon binding to NPs are easily recognized by immune cells leading to their clearance from the body [25]. Therefore, hybridization of polymer NPs with liposomes solves the above-mentioned limitations and results in the more stable structures with good biocompatibility and bioavailability, and controlled release of substances. This is achieved by encapsulation of polymer core into the liposome and the addition of PEG to the outer lipid layer. The inner lipid layer prevents polymer from degradation by acting as a water barrier (Fig. 7.1 ) [23].

LPHNs are mainly used in the anticancer therapy to act as vehicles for delivering drugs, genetic material, and vaccines. The encapsulation strategies and the preparation methods vary greatly, which affects their size, stability, efficiency for substance integration, and substance release rate [19].

There are three strategies implemented in the development of LPHNs: conventional two-step method, nonconventional two-step method, and a one-step method (Fig. 7.1) [23]. Initially, LPHNs have been produced using the conventional two-step method that employs preformed polymer NPs and preformed liposomes, which are being mixed together to form polymer–lipid suspension. The suspension then undergoes vortexing or sonication at higher temperature than that required for transition of gel to the liquid. The process is followed by centrifugation to separate LPHNs from the suspension. Preformed polymer NPs are created with any of the three methods: emulsification–solvent–evaporation (ESE) [26,27], nanoprecipitation [28,29], or high-pressure homogenization [30]. The obtained polymer NPs are then mixed with preformed liposomes or dried lipid film. Different combinations and ratios of polymer and lipids were demonstrated to work as hybrid NPs. Among them, polymers such as maltodextrin, PLGA, and polystyrene were coated with lipids such as cholesterol or dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine [31].

The nonconventional method utilizes the same principle of mixing preformed liposomes and polymer NPs but uses different methods such as spray drying or soft lithography particle molding, which is also known as particle replication in non-wetting templates [32].

Due to the limitations of the two-step methods such as predetermined size and pre-formed particles, expensive and time-consuming process, a new strategy gained popularity. The one-step method is easier and faster as it skips the step of separate preparation of particles and rather simultaneously mixes polymers and lipid to self-assemble a single hybrid entity. This could be conducted using either nanoprecipitation or ESE methods. ESE is able to produces larger LPHNs (~200–300nm in size) and therefore it allows encapsulation of larger range of substances with higher efficiency. In comparison to ESE, nanoprecipitation offers better physical characteristics such as size homogeneity and even liposome coverage, which are advantageous in terms of large-scale production prospects [29].

The hybrid structure of LPHNs allows the combinatorial delivery of substances such as drugs, vaccines, genetic material, radioisotopes, and magnetic NPs for theranostic applications. Different strategies have been developed for incorporation of multiple substances to LPHNs (Fig. 7.2 ). The above-mentioned substances can be encapsulated into LPHNs, covalently attached to either polymer or lipid precursors, incorporated into preformed polymer NPs or liposomes, or can be conjugated to the outer layer of LPHNs that already contains a different substance inside its core. Thus, LPHNs are able to carry several substances at once by binding them to the inner and outer layers.

Majority of LPHNs are developed for use in cancer therapy and diagnosis. They were shown to have higher concentration and cytotoxicity within cancer cells due to the greater cellular uptake and extended drug exposure in comparison to the free drug [23,33].

Liposomes with inorganic substances such as paramagnetic iron oxides, gold NPs, and QD are widely used in medical imaging systems. These particles have magnetic and optical properties that allow them to be detected with MRI in vivo. But the low colloidal stability and hydrophobicity restrict their use in clinical settings. Therefore, combining these particles together with liposomes offers better stability in plasma and the ability to observe the release of active substances. Particles were shown to be incorporated into the core of the liposomes, its bilayer, and the surface [33]. Some of the developed hybrid liposome NPs possess theranostic activity, which was successful in the combination of QD embedded in bilayer or conjugated to the liposome surface with incorporated doxorubicin (DOX) [34,35], apomorphin QD [36], or gold NPs [37] adsorbed to the liposome surface with incorporated pDNA [38,39], and QD adsorbed to the liposome surface and incorporated siRNA [40].

Gold NPs have demonstrated potential for optical and photothermal (PT) heating. To this aim, they can be either incorporated into the liposome core (if they are hydrophilic) or linked to the outer layer of liposomes (in case they are hydrophobic). Liposomes fused with gold NPs can be traced in the dynamic environment due to the strong light scattered from the gold NPs (Fig. 7.3 ) [41].

Hydrophilic magnetic particles can be incorporated into the core of liposomes or onto the surface, while hydrophobic magnetic nanocrystals are usually embedded into the liposome bilayer [19]. Magnetic nanocrystals linked with liposomes are used not only for MRI but could also be used as theranostics when heated. Electromagnetic heating allows a controlled release of active substances due to the increase permeability of the liposome bilayer and its partial rupture [42].

Similar to magnetic nanocrystals, hydrophilic QD are either linked with the outer layer of liposomes or encapsulated in the core, while hydrophobic QD are incorporated into the liposomal membrane. In comparison to liposomes linked with gold NPs or magnetic nanocrystals, liposome fused with QD behave differently depending on the PEGylation state. For examples, PEGylated liposomes release QD into the cell membrane, whereas non-PEGylated liposomes are absorbed into cell interior together with QD [19,43].

Micellar hybrid NPs have cylindrical shape that consists of amphiphilic polymers containing hydrophobic and hydrophilic blocks. Micelles are colloidal NPs of 5–50nm in size. Due to relatively small size, micelles are able to penetrate cancer tissues more efficiently with an even distribution across the tumor site. They are able to encapsulate hydrophobic or amphiphilic substances in its core that consist of hydrophobic tails. When hydrophilic part of micelles faces aqueous environment, it orients to the aqueous region, thus stabilizing the colloidal assembly (Fig. 7.4 ) [19,44].

Micelles enable a control over distribution, targeting, and release of active substances due to the relative stability and a cylindrical form that provides a large space for encapsulation. Similarly to liposomes, combination of micelles with nanocrystals produces stable entities that allow dynamic imaging of drug release in vivo. Moreover, covering of nanocrystals with PE-PEG (diacyl phospholipid-PEG) leads to both higher stability of micelles and to the increased uptake of hydrophobic substances [19]. In addition, coating of magnetic nanocrystals with lipids was shown to be more beneficial to polymer coating in terms of prolonged bioavailability and delivery of active substances to the tumor site [45].

In contrast to liposomes, micelles are able to carry several payloads along with the active substance. For example, micelles were loaded with nanocrystals and QD and DOX. This enables unique theranostic application of hybrid micelles NPs in obtaining data from the tumor site using both MRI and fluorescence imaging [46]. Encapsulation of QD and gold NPs in micelles’ center and edges, respectively, allows their precise positioning required for increasing of photoluminescence of QD and preventing any disruptions in emission from gold NPs. This multiloading of micelles can be achieved using several methods including coprecipitation, templated cooperative assembly, heating and cooling, and interfacial instabilities of emulsion droplets [47]. But, of all the methods, hydrogen bonding directed supramolecular assembly allowed control over localization and loading of metal NPs in the micelles [48]. This was achieved by coating gold NPs with a homopolymer or binary mixed ligands that directed metal NPs in the micellar environment. Ligand-coated metal NPs were prepared using ligand-exchange method. The modification of the surface of the metal NPs is required for enhanced stability and regulation of entropy and enthalpy of micelle formation. Self-assembly of hybrid micelle NPs is affected by the enthalpy contribution of the NP–polymer interaction and the entropy loss of the polymer resulted from the deformation of the polymer chain induced by the insertion of NPs. Localization of gold NPs was shown to depend on the coating ligand. Thus, coating with homopolymer ligand resulted in the localization of metal NPs in the core of the micelle, while the use of binary mixed ligand favored the localization to the interface between the micellar blocks.

Moreover, coating of micelles has demonstrated a separated and controlled release of the incorporated active substances. For example, upon accumulation at the tumor site, the outer layer of micelles releases angiogenesis inhibiting drug, and after that the polymeric core releases anticancer drug. This unique two-in-one action enables more complex and hierarchical targeting of tumor sites in comparison to hybrid liposomal NPs [49,50].

Additionally, hybrid polymer micelles were developed with the ability to response to specific stimuli such as pH, light, temperature, and oxidized and reduced conditions [51–54]. More recent advances involve a response of hybrid polymer–micelle NPs to enzymes [55]. The feature of responsiveness to enzymes was selected because certain enzymes were shown to be associated with some diseases. Thus, hybrid micelles were designed to disassemble and release active substances upon the contact with a certain enzyme found at the disease site. The hybrid NPs consisted of PEG attached to dendrons with hydrophobic end-groups. The end-groups react to enzymatic hydrolysis leading to disassembly of hybrid NPs and release of active substances. The length of PEG was shown to affect stability of micelles, where longer PEG chains provided higher stability.

Although, a self-assembling property of micelles has been exploited widely, its spontaneous dissociation has been considered as a drawback. This dissociation usually occurs when critical micelle concentration is extremely low as in case of the dilution in the bloodstream. To avoid the dissociation, various strategies of cross-linking have been applied. Small organic molecules such as diamines and cystamine were used for cross-linking and stabilizing micelles [56]. However, due to toxicity, expensive, and time-consuming process, they have not reached the market. Therefore, inorganic silica-based materials have been utilized to protect micelles from diffusion and provide stability [57].

Viral hybrid NPs are able to provide a better selectivity and efficient transfection for delivering nucleic acids for therapeutic purposes. Different types of viruses have been utilized as NPs. The advantages of using viruses lie in the better selectivity and the possibility to genetically reengineer them to suit the desired purposes. The size and shape of viral NPs allows more efficient encapsulation of nucleic acids with therapeutic effects. Viruses used for this purpose come in a variety of shapes and sizes such as rod-shaped TMV (Tobacco Mosaic Virus) and PVX (Potato Virus X), spherical, disk and wedges CCMV (Cowpea Chlorotic Mottle Virus), and Cowpea Mosaic Virus [58–61]. For example, a rod shape of CCMV has a larger payload capacity than spherical particles. Moreover, the longer the rod, the more flexible the particle is, as in the case of TMV, which has short and wide structure that makes it more rigid [62]. Interestingly, viruses exhibit change in the original shape depending on the environmental conditions such as pH. A simple variation in pH can trigger a shift in the charge of side chains from charged to neutral and back.

Viral NPs are also capable of self-assembly, which is an essential property to create hybrid nanoplatforms. To achieve this, a strong preference such as electrostatic interaction between different entities is required. Along with compatibility, both counterparts need to have complementary reactive groups. The latter was demonstrated on the addition of the complementary DNA fragments to both the Qβ-phage capsid and inorganic NPs [63]. Modification of electrostatic interaction was shown by negatively charging the surface of inorganic NPs, which allowed capsid proteins to coat inorganic surface. This can also be an example of the size effect on the assembling routes. In case of inorganic nanomaterials, viral NPs assemble around it and vice versa. However, when both entities have a similar size, larger hybrid assemblies occur (Fig. 7.5 ) [64].

Combination of viral NPs with polymers lowers degradation rate and enhances interfacial activity. Polymers can be attached to viral NPs through covalent and noncovalent interactions. For example, CPMV and TMV are combined with electrolyte polymers and polyaniline through noncovalent bonding [65]. This particular combination revealed that the assembling of viral NPs onto the polymers depends on their size. Moreover, concentration of viral NPs in the solution affected assembling morphology. Lower concentration of TMV led to the formation of elongated structures, while higher concentration resulted in the branched morphology.

Similar to the case with liposomal and micellar nanosystems, viral NPs were also shown to be linked to magnetic nanocrystals and metal nanoplatforms to obtain additional imaging advantages of the therapeutic compounds. Thus, QDs were placed either on the surface of the viral capsid or inside its core. The combination of QDs with adenovirus generated hybrid NPs capable of selectively targeting cells containing Coxsackie virus B adenovirus receptor and efficiently delivering active substances [19]. Attachment of gold NPs to the specifically modified surface of adenovirus provided additional hyperthermia function. Gold NPs were covalently bonded to lysine residues on the viral capsid surface. Moreover, gold-viral hybrid NPs can be activated by PT heating either by attaching C60 or by incorporating viral particles into gold NPs [66].

Bacterial viruses have also been utilized as hybrid nanosystems. Specifically bacteriophage M13 was genetically engineered to be assembled with carbon nanotubes to be visualized in cancer cells [67]. Additionally, the bacteriophage can be further modified to bear therapeutic genes and to contain target ligand on its surface [19]. A slightly different example of virus modification is PVX, which was engineered with a peptide sequence YSDQPTQSSQRP. The peptide covered the surface of the virus and stimulated formation of the silicon dioxide on the viral surface [62]. Moreover, phage M13 was used to stabilize drug carrying liposomes [68]. This was achieved by attaching ZnPc (zinc phthalocyanine)-modified liposomes that had positive charge onto the genetically engineered bacteriophage that contained negatively charged side chains on its surface. Such a combination strategy was used to increase the fluorescence of the hybrid NPs.

Silica-based NPs have drawn much attention due to their exceptional biophysical properties, such as reported low toxicity, nanoscale dimensions, proven biocompatibility, high surface area (that can be exploited for its modification), capacity of encapsulation and combination with various materials, and excellent optical characteristics [69,70]. These unique features enabled the application of silica NPs in a variety of biomedical areas, including but not limited to, drug delivery [71–74], PDT [75,76], cell imaging [77], biosensor systems [78,79], and enzyme immobilization and delivery [80,81].

Compared to pure silica nanoconstructs, mesoporous platform provides a leverage for nanomedical applications thanks to enlarged active surface area, high pore volume, and enhanced tunability [82]. Due to optimal pore sizes and biocompatibility, mesoporous silica NPs are considered as an ideal candidate for drug delivery and gene transfection [83–85]. Usually mesoporous silica NPs have been fabricated using a template-directed method, first described by Cai et al. [86], where the synthesis occurs in the presence of a supramolecular assembled surfactant acting as a template. However, despite excellent biochemical and biophysical properties, the biomedical application of mesoporous silica NPs has been hampered by a few factors such as biodegradability and long-term biocompatibility [87]. This fact nudged the researchers to develop more effective and safe mesoporous silica-based nanosystems. To improve biodegradability, Hao and co-workers developed hybrid mesoporous silica-based drug carrier nanoplatform by using hydroxyapatite (Fig. 7.6 ) [88]. The researchers fabricated degradable and pH-responsive mesoporous silica NPs/hydroxyapatite hybrid drug carrier by homogeneously incorporating calcium salt into silica NPs. The resulted hybrid NPs exhibited a drug loading capacity about five times higher than that of ordinary silica NPs. The degradation of NPs has been evaluated through in vitro and in vivo experiments. The data of biological experiments demonstrated that hybrid mesoporous silica NPs/hydroxyapatite exhibited a good biocompatibility and antitumor effect. Moreover, it was showed that hybrid NPs possess 2.4 times higher loading capacity for DOX (anticancer agent) compared to standard silica NPs. Due to pH-responsive drug release property, hybrid NPs demonstrated exceptionally high and sustainable release of loaded DOX (eight times higher than ordinary silica NPs). In addition, treatment of mice with a model of human breast cancer by DOX-loaded hybrid NPs was superior (38.8% comparing to DOX-loaded silica NPs).

Another solution for pH-responsive hybrid mesoporous silica NPs for targeted drug delivery has been proposed by Theron et al. [89]. To enhance targeting capacity, pH-sensitive hybrid mesoporous silica NPs loaded with dyes have been functionalized with molecular recognition sites by anchoring a triazine/uracil fragment on the surface. The rational of the study was that pores in hybrid NPs are sealed at blood pH (7.4) to prevent premature drug release, and they will be open at the acidic pH of lysosomes (5.5–4.5) to deliver the loaded molecules (propidium iodide, rhodamine, and camptothecin). The results of in vitro studies indicated that the hybrid mesoporous silica NPs were taken up by MCF-7 cells and localized in the acidic lysosome as a result of opening of the pores and the dye release. The cytotoxicity studies on human breast cancer cells demonstrated a decrease in cell viability similar to that of the drug alone, confirming the drug release after pore opening in the acidic environment.

Wu and co-workers developed hybrid silica NPs densely grafted with well-defined thermo- and pH dual-responsive brushes via surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) (Fig. 7.7 ) [90]. ATRP initiators were attached on the surface of poly (N-isopropylacrylamide)-b-poly(4-vinylpyridine)-grafted silica NPs followed by ATRP of N-isopropylacrylamide. The chain extension with 4-vinylpyridine from the fabricated hybrid grafted NPs was achieved. The quaternization of the outer 4-vinylpyridine block with methyl iodide allowed hybrid NPs to be well dispersed in aqueous solution. It provides an opportunity to scrutinize the pH- and thermo-responsive behavior of the di-block copolymer chains on the hybrid NPs surface.

Wang et al. reported on the development of novel hybrid multidrug delivery system based on mechanized nanoplatform fabricated through functionalization of mesoporous silica NPs with acid-cleavable intermediate linkages and monoferrocene functionalized β-cyclodextrin as supramolecular nanovalves (metal–organic frameworks) [91]. The controlled release of incorporated anticancer drugs (gemcitabine and DOX) was achieved using the application of voltage and acid stimuli. Cell viability experiments showed that proposed hybrid multidrug delivery system possessed an improved cytotoxicity to cancer MCF-7 cells in comparison with single drug-loaded mechanized silica NPs. Jo et al. showed that coordination polymerization of organic building blocks and metal nodes can be initiated on carboxylate-terminated silica particles to form silica-coordination polymer core-shell microspheres [92]. Moreover, they demonstrated that the shell thickness could be controlled by adjusting the amount of reactants such as coordination polymer precursors and silica particles (Fig. 7.8 ).

The high potential of mesoporous silica NPs for delivery of anticancer compounds encouraged researches to find an opportunity to improve efficacy of molecular delivery and target capabilities. Ma et al. fabricated hollow mesoporous silica NPs coated with FA-conjugated polyethyleneimine under neutral conditions through electrostatic interactions between the partially charged amino groups of polyethyleneimine and the phosphate groups on the NPs surfaces [93]. This platform has been employed for drug/siRNA co-delivery, where FA played a role of targeting ligand. The study showed that coated hollow mesoporous silica NPs possessed an enhanced siRNA binding capability on account of electrostatic interactions between the amino groups of polyethyleneimine and siRNA compared with noncoated counterparts. The data of in vitro experiments showed pH-responsive drug/siRNA selective delivery to HeLa cells via FA receptor–mediated cellular endocytosis. The researchers demonstrated a feasibility of combined delivery anticancer agent (DOX) and siRNA against the B-cell lymphoma 2 (Bcl-2) protein into the HeLa cells resulting in apoptosis induction (through the suppression of anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2). The same research group reported on development of a redox-responsive drug/siRNA nanoplatform based on mesoporous silica NPs for co-delivery siRNA and DOX in vitro and in vivo (Fig. 7.9 ) [94]. The surface of mesoporous silica NPs was functionalized with the adamantine providing a possibility of forming of stable host–guest complex between disulfide bond-linked adamantine and ethylenediamine-modified β-cyclodextrin to seal loaded drugs inside the pores. At the same time, amino groups were able of forming a complex with siRNA through electrostatic interaction. The results of in vitro studies revealed that delivery of Bcl-2 siRNA led to silence of Bcl-2 protein expression in HeLa cells. In vivo DOX delivery (on transgenic zebrafish larvae) led to the reduction of liver tumor.

In another study, for co-delivery of anticancer drug and siRNA, functionalized mesoporous silica NPs were developed [95]. To improve targeted co-delivery and achieve synergetic anticancer effect, mesoporous silica NPs were functionalized with copolymer (polyethylenimine-PEG), and then loaded with epirubicin hydrochloride and anti Bcl-2 siRNA’s. The data of in vitro and in vivo experiments indicated that functionalized mesoporous silica NPs loaded with drug and siRNA exhibited better antitumor activity compared to NPs containing epirubicin and siRNA alone.

The impact of design, type of functional group, and presence of serum on DOX delivery by mesoporous silica NPs was investigated by Shahabi et al. [96]. In this study, researchers fabricated NPs of with four different functional groups: amine, sulfonate, PEG (or polyethylene imine), and one type of antibody-conjugated NPs for cell targeting too. The fabricated hybrid mesoporous silica NPs were characterized regarding their physicochemical properties, colloidal stability, cellular uptake, and release of DOX by human osteosarcoma cells; and cytotoxic potential of hybrid mesoporous silica NPs on cancer cells as well. The acquired data indicated that the best approach that allowed both spatial enhancement over DOX loading and in vitro DOX delivery was sulfonate functionalization of mesoporous silica NPs. It was found out that sulfonate functionalization led in enhanced attachment of DOX to the pores of NPs with the highest negative zeta potential by electrostatic interactions. Moreover, the highest nuclear concentration of DOX was observed with sulfonate-functionalized mesoporous silica NPs resulted in the highest antiproliferative and cytotoxic effects. Tan et al. analyzed the influence of chain length, type of terminal groups, density of disulfide-appended functional ligands on the surface of mesoporous silica NPs on drug-loading capacity, and glutathione-triggered drug-release kinetics [97]. The researchers demonstrated that ligand with an intermediate length and a bulky terminal group possessed the highest drug-loading capacity as well as good release kinetics. It was also revealed that decreasing the surface coverage of the functional ligands results in an increase of drug release.

Another perspective direction for biomedical applications of hybrid mesoporous silica NPs is the development of enzyme-mediated controlled release platforms. In these systems, the enzymes would play a role of “keys” for triggering drug delivery or local biological reactions. Candel et al. synthesized MCM-41 mesoporous silica NPs, which were loaded with biological stain (Safranine O) [98]. The external surface of hybrid NPs was functionalized with N-(3-triethoxysilylpropyl) gluconamide. Such a design allows formation of dense hydrogen-bonded network around the pore outlets, i.e., sealing the pores and preventing untimely drug delivery. However, in the presence of the amidase, the amide bond is hydrolyzed resulting in the reduction in size of the attached capping molecule and, consequently, the release of loaded material. Candel and coworkers observed delivery of Safranine O dye upon the addition of enzymes (amidase and pronase) which was caused by the enzymatic hydrolysis of the amide bond in the anchored gluconamide derivative. As the next step, the researchers fabricated MCM-41 mesoporous silica NPs loaded with anticancer drug camptothecin and capped with the gluconamide derivative. The culture of HeLa cells was employed to demonstrate the cellular internalization of fabricated hybrid NPs, drug release after lysosomal localization, and uncapping of pores by lysosomal enzymes. The cytotoxic effect in the group treated by hybrid NPs loaded with camptothecin was significantly higher comparing to the group exposed to hybrid NPs alone.

Bernardos et al. reported on successful synthesis of the gate-like functional hybrid systems consisted of MCM-41-based nanomaterials functionalized on the pore outlets with different “saccharide” derivatives and a dye [99]. The hybrid mesoporous silica NPs S1, S2, and S3 containing the grafted starch derivatives Glucidex 47, Glucidex 39, and Glucidex 29 were synthesized. The anchoring of these saccharide derivatives was made to prevent cargo delivery due to the formation, around the pore outlets, of a dense hydrogen-bonding saccharide network. The authors hypothesized that in the presence of enzymes such as pancreatin or β-D-galactosidase, the hydrolysis of the saccharide network results in an uncapping of the pores in hybrid silica NPs, resulting in release of the entrapped molecule. Then, hybrid NPs of Type S1 were studied for the controlled release of dye and internalization in the cells. Moreover, mesoporous silica NPs functionalized with Glucidex 47 and loaded with DOX were fabricated. It was shown that cell viability with DOX-loaded NPs decreased due to the specific internalization of the NPs, enzyme-dependent opening of the saccharide molecular gate, and controlled drug delivery. Later, the same research group has employed two different anchoring strategies to develop enzyme-responsive intracellular-controlled release system based on using silica mesoporous NPs capped with ε-poly-L-lysine [100]. First strategy involved the random formation of urea bonds by the treatment of propyl isocyanate-functionalized MCM-41 NPs with the lysine amino groups located on the ε-poly-L-lysine backbone. The second approach was based on a specific attachment through the carboxyl terminus of the polypeptide with azidopropyl-functionalized MCM-41 NPs. Both types of hybrid NPs demonstrated a zero release of loaded molecules in water due to the protective coverage of the surface by polymer ε-poly-L-lysine. At the same time, researchers detected a delivery in the presence of proteases caused by hydrolysis of polymer amide bonds.

Lin et al. designed hybrid mesoporous silica nanosystem with attached trans-activating transcriptional activator (TAT)-fusion denatured enzyme to enhance the permeability of cell membrane for antioxidant enzymes [81]. This novel two-enzyme delivery system demonstrated synergistic effect for protecting cells against an oxidative stress and cell cycle arrest. The study demonstrated an ability of hybrid multifunctional system to regulate intracellular anti–reactive oxygen species (ROS) cascade reactions by delivery image fluorophore and antioxidant enzymes.

The issues of biocompatibility and drug loading capacity of silica-based nanocarriers urged the scientists to explore new organic–inorganic combinations and methods of coating. For example, Yang et al. proposed to utilize bovine serum albumin (BSA)-coated fluorescent organic–inorganic hybrid silica NPs to enhance drug delivery efficiency (Fig. 7.10 ) [101]. The silica NPs were conjugated with dimethyl-3,3’-(((4-formylphenyl)azanediyl)bis-(4,1-phenylene))diacrylate (DNDT) to monitor the interactions between NPs and the protein molecules. The resulting fluorescent silica NPs display very limited dispersion ability in the aqueous phase. The DTNT acted as a sensitive monitor to express signals when the biomacromolecule BSA was coated onto the surfaces of hybrid NPs. The well-established DNDT and SiO2–NH2@BSA is water dispersible, which is very helpful for the uniform and high concentration cargo loading. The hyperthermia-induced molecular delivery by DNDT and SiO2–NH2@BSA NPs was demonstrated.

A feasibility of synthesis of hybrid organic–inorganic silica NPs templated from elastin-like polypeptide (ELP) micelles was demonstrated by Han et al. [102]. The hybrid silica–polypeptide NPs were formed by combining the temperature-triggered self-assembly of ELP into monodisperse micelles with the silicification properties of the silaffin R5 peptide in water. It was shown that the incorporation of the silaffin R5 peptide at the hydrophilic terminus of the diblock ELP results in the presentation of the silaffin R5 peptide on the coronae of the micelles leading to localized condensation of silica and the formation of near-monodisperse hybrid ELP–silica NPs.

Ge et al. developed novel type of organic–inorganic hybrid silica NPs with contact antibacterial properties for application in ultraviolet (UV)-curable coatings [103]. The study demonstrated that silicon dioxide-dimethyl-pentaerythritol triacrylate (SiO2–DMx–PETAx) NPs modified with quaternary ammonium species possess good dispersity and antibacterial ability. The tests conducted on Gram-negative Escherichia coli and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus cultures showed excellent antibacterial properties of SiO2–DM50–PETA50 NPs. In addition, it was found out that soybean oil-based coating improved mechanical properties of NPs, including gloss, pencil hardness, pendulum hardness, and crosshatch adhesion; and moreover, it had antibacterial activity against both E. coli and S. aureus. Apart from the study of Ge et al. [103], there are few reports on successful application of modified hybrid silica-based NPs as antibacterial agents [104–108].

The good biocompatibility, excellent optical transparency, versatile chemistry of silica NPs and the surface negative charge provides an opportunity for surface modification and conjugation for cell tracking and imaging [109]. Zhang et al. emphasized that silica NPs can be easily modified with a variety of functional groups, and they can encapsulate a range of dyes and fluorescent molecules without imparting their optical characteristics [110]. There are two different methods for synthesis silica-based NPs: Stöber method and micro-emulsion method. Stöber method allows fabrication of NPs from 50nm to 2μm with a varying degree of sphericity and monodispersity. Some research groups have utilized Stöber method to fabricate ultra-bright core-shell NP C dots [111]; large stokes-shift fluorescent silica NPs with enhanced emission [112]; silica NPs with incorporated calcium [113]; and monodispersed luminescent silica NPs for bioanalytical assays [114]. However, some fluorescent markers such as trisodium 8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,6-trisulfonate are not compatible with Stöber method of synthesis [115]. Compared to Stöber technique, microemulsion method provides a superior monodispersity of silica NPs [116]. This method was successfully employed to synthesize a variety of silica NPs loaded with oxygen-sensitive dyes and cellular markers [110,117–120].

One of the recent trends is developing dye-doped fluorescent silica NPs for biomedical applications due to their excellent characteristics, including biocompatibility, nontoxicity, high hydrophilicity, and optical transparency [121–123]. Santra et al. produced luminescent NPs of various sizes made of inorganic luminescent dye, Tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) dichlororuthenium (II) hexahydrate, doped inside a silica network using a water-in-oil microemulsion method [124]. The surface of dye-doped fluorescent silica NPs has been biochemically modified to attach membrane-anchoring groups and stain human leukemia cells. In another work, spherical silica NPs coated with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) dyes were fabricated using an improved Stöber method (Fig. 7.11 ) [125]. It was shown that photostability of FITC-doped silica NPs was higher compared to the free FITC in the solution. The results of cell imaging demonstrated that the FITC-doped silica NPs could label the non-small-cell lung cancer, and they could be used as fluorescent probes for diagnostic applications.

Tran et al. synthesized dye-doped water soluble organically modified silica (ORMOSIL) NPs by using modified Stöber method from methyltriethoxysilane CH3Si(OCH3)3 precursor [126]. The surfaces of NPs were modified with BSA, biocompatible chemical reagents, and then, combined with specific bacterial and breast cancer antigen antibodies. These novel dye-doped silica-based NPs were used for immunolabeling the bacteria E. coli O157:H7. The breast cancer cells were labeled by anti-HER2 monoclonal antibody (mAb)–NPs complex and optically imaged and detected with much higher sensibility than those labeled by antibody-free dyes. Nooney et al. prepared silica NPs doped with far-red fluorescent cyanine dyes, Cy5 and FR670, using the microemulsion method [127]. The synthesis of NPs loaded with dye was achieved using an intramicellar nucleation pathway combined with a negatively charged surfactant. The NPs were coated with a silica shell utilizing either the microemulsion or the Stöber method. Cy5 dye-doped NPs were tested for labeling and detection C-reactive protein (biomarker for inflammation and cardiovascular disease).

Apart from cell tracking and fluorescent imaging, silica-based nanosystems can be effectively employed for MRI as well. To this aim, contrast agents can be incorporated into core or shell of silica NPs to improve their relaxivity, biocompatibility, and prolong circulation time. It was shown that mesoporous silica NPs might be considered as ideal nanoplatform for the development of MR-enhancing hybrid nanomaterials thank to their tunable pore structures and large surface area that can be modified to improve targeted delivery [128–131]. Taylor et al. synthesized mesoporous silica NPs coated with a Gd–Si-DTTA complex via the siloxane linkage by refluxing the particles and the Gd–Si-DTTA complex in toluene (Fig. 7.12 ) [128]. The fabricated NPs exhibited a very large longitudinal and transvers relaxivities proven on 3.0T and 9.4T MR-scanner. Data of in vitro studies conducted on murine monocyte cells showed the efficient uptake of mesoporous silica NPs loaded with Gd. In addition, T1 and T2 weighted enhancement has been detected during in vivo experiments on mice.

Gd-incorporated MCM-41 mesoporous silica NPs have been synthesized by a one-step method [132]. The water-adsorptive capabilities of these hybrid NPs as contrast agents were evaluated by molecular dynamic simulation and in experiments on mice. The NPs were dispersed within the liver and tumor organs of the mice injected with the NP solution. It was found that Gd2O3@MCM-41 NPs were capable enhancing in vivo T1-weighted images of the mice in nasopharyngeal carcinoma xenografted CNE-2 tumors and inferior vena cava.

Peng et al. reported on the development of multifunctional mesoporous silica hollow manganese oxide NPs for use as a novel theranostic nanoplatform, particularly for targeted optical imaging, T-1 MRI, and PDT [133]. The iron (III) complex was incorporated into NPs for the phosphorescence imaging and PDT. Then, the shell surface of hybrid mesoporous silica NPs was functionalized with FA to improve cancer targeting properties. The results of combined confocal microscopy and MRI studies clearly indicated that the hybrid NPs are able of specifically targeting cancer cells via the overexpressing folate receptors.

Earlier, in 2006, Lee and co-workers proposed a new type of hybrid “core-satellite” dual functional NPs composed of dye-doped “core” and multiple “satellites” of magnetic NPs [134]. The researchers synthesized rhodamine dye–doped silica NPs with surface amine groups and IONPs coated with 2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid. Then, both types of NPs were conjugated to form “core-satellite” hybrid nanoplatform. The resulted NPs showed an increased T2 relaxivity coefficient and improvement of MR signal compared to free magnetic NPs. Moreover, hybrid NPs exhibited an enhanced fluorescence behavior too. As the next step, the researchers demonstrated the feasibility of utilization of hybrid nanosystem for simultaneous optical and MR imaging of neuroblastoma cells overexpressing polysialic acids by conjugating them with HmenB1 antibodies.

Aside from being exclusively considered as a nano-vehicle for anticancer drug delivery, mesoporous silica NPs can be effectively modified into theranostic platform for simultaneous enhanced MRI, fluorescence imaging, and drug delivery as well. Nakamura et al. developed hybrid NPs composed of a perfluoro-15-crown-5-ether core and mesoporous silica shell with encapsulated highly sensitive 19F MRI marker. Then, the hybrid NPs were labeled with fluorescent dyes and folate functionalized for active targeting [135]. The effective cell uptake of NPs was confirmed by confocal laser scanning microscopy, flow cytometry, and MR imaging. In addition, anticancer drug (DOX) was loaded into hybrid NPs followed by the evaluation of the release profiles of encapsulated drug at different pH values by UV-visible absorption spectroscopy. The drug release was observed in the endosome and lysosome at pH 5.0–5.5. These findings can be applicable for cancer treatment, because the pH of malignant cells is lower compared to normal cells. The researchers concluded that the proposed novel hybrid platform has an advantage over current drug delivery systems due to intrinsic low background signals of 19F MRI.

Magnetite nanocrystals can be encapsulated into silica-based nanosystems to enhance MRI signal. Lee et al. synthesized highly versatile nanocomposite NPs by decorating the surface of mesoporous dye-doped silica NPs with multiple magnetite nanocrystals [136]. The researchers developed a theranostic platform allowing simultaneous enhanced MRI due to the synergistic magnetism, in vitro imaging using Rhodamine B isothiocyanate dye, and anticancer activity caused by DOX. Wu et al. encapsulated multiple magnetite nanocrystals into PEGylated silica NPs for high-performance microscopic MR angiography [137].

As an alternative to using solely mesoporous silica particles to optimize MRI, Joshi et al. proposed to utilize the non-porous SiO2 NPs [138]. The authors hypothesized that such a design provides a broad access for water molecules to gadolinium molecules, which is critical for a suitable contrast enhancement. Joshi et al. argued that in porous structures, gadolinium molecules are enclosed into the pores of the silica NPs limiting the diffusion processes and functionality. The external surface of NPs was functionalized by Gd-DO3A hexylamine (Gd-DO3A-HA) as MRI contrast agent, a cell-penetrating peptide (octaarginine, R 8) to enhance the cellular uptake (in vitro), and fluorophores for optical imaging in vivo. The results showed a high relaxivity per particle and good cellular uptake by 3T3 mouse fibroblasts. Nevertheless, it was revealed that NPs were tending to form larger agglomerates in a biological environment hampering the uptake by the cultured cells.

Polysilsesquioxane NPs (PSilQ) are new type of hybrid nanoplatforms that emerged last decade. These hybrid NPs are fabricated using the condensation of silanol-based monomers where Si molecule plays a role of metal-connecting point [1]. Compared to classic silica-based nanosystems, PSilQ NPs provide an opportunity for high drug loading exclusively on their surface. Due to their high tunability and drug loading capacity, PSilQ NPs have been a subject of numerous studies and applications.

The main focus for the application of PSilQ NPs as drug carrier nanosystem is cancer treatment. Rocca and coworkers reported on the production NPs composed of PSilQ polymer cross-linked by a cisplatin prodrug (cisplatin-PSilQ) for the utilization in chemo-radiotherapy on non-small-cell lung cancer model [139]. It was showed that PSilQ NPs possess an exceptionally high loading capacity for cisplatin. Cisplatin-PSilQ NPs demonstrated significantly higher therapeutic efficacy when compared to cisplatin applied alone. Earlier, the same group reported on synthesis of hybrid PSilQ NPs with loaded oxaliplatin (platinum-based chemotherapeutic) (Fig. 7.13 ) [140]. The NPs were functionalized by two surface functional groups—silanol groups and carboxylic acids for postsynthesis grafting of cyclic arginine–glycine–aspartate peptide and the anisamide ligand for targeting cancer cells. In addition, the NPs were also modified with PEG to stabilize the NP structure and improve its half-life. The results of in vitro studies demonstrated the increased uptake of hybrid NPs by human colon cancer cells through integrin receptor targeting and by human pancreatic cancer cells via sigma receptor targeting. In addition, DLD-1 colon cancer cells showed increased cellular localization and uptake of arginine-glycine-aspartic sequence (RGD)–rhod-1 particles compared to the untargeted rhod-1 particles. Aside from that, the impact of oxaliplatin-PSilQ NPs on tumor growth inhibition was assessed on a murine model of pancreatic cancer. It revealed that mice treated by hybrid NPs had larger necrotic regions compared to the “oxaliplatin-only”–treated animals.

Vega et al. developed stimulus-responsive photosensitizer delivery systems based on PSilQ NPs for PDT [141]. PSilQ NPs were fabricated by a reverse microemulsion method using 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(carboxyphenyl) porphyrin silane derivatives. The results of the study demonstrated successful redox-responsive release of photosensitizer molecules in the presence of a reducing agent. The data of phototoxicity in vitro experiments showed that the redox-responsive hybrid PSilQ NPs induced an enhanced phototherapeutic effect on HeLa cervical cancer cells compared to nonresponsive counterparts. The authors hypothesized that this phenomenon could be caused by the efficient transport and intracellular release of photosensitizer agents. In another study dedicated to PDT, bridged silsesquioxane (SilQ) nanocomposites with tunable morphologies incorporating o-nitrophenylene–ammonium bridges were fabricated [142]. The equal distribution of SilQs and organic content in the particles procured photo-responsive properties. The unique composition of these SilQ NPs allows on-demand surface charge reversal using light triggering from positive to negative charges. Moreover, it allowed light-triggered plasmid DNA delivery in cancer cells without any functionalization of the surface.

Apart from treatment purposes, it has also been demonstrated that PSilQ NPs can be utilized for cancer imaging. Vivero-Escoto et al. synthesized two novel bis(trialkoxysilyl) derivatives of Gadolinium (Gd III) diethylenetriamine pentaacetate containing disulfide linkages to form biodegradable Gd-PSilQ NPs by base-catalyzed condensation reactions in reverse microemulsions [143]. The relaxivity for the Gd-PSilQ NPs was measured with a 3T MR scanner. The results demonstrated that r1 relaxivities of the Gd-PSilQ NPs was better compared to other NP-based MRI contrast agents, and they are superior to the contrast agent Magnevist (Bayer, AG). Then, the effectiveness of Gd-PSilQ NPs as T1-weighted MRI contrast agents was showed in vitro on a 3T MR scanner using cancer cells, and it was confirmed by confocal laser scanning microscopy.

Polymeric hybrid NPs can be represented as a class of NPs-containing polymers as the organic component [144]. The polymer component typically has structural functions and may tailor the mechanical features and production of the final materials. The inorganic component is used for specific functionality (catalytic activity, luminescence, imaging, magnetism, etc.) and/or for enhancement of mechanical and thermal properties of the polymer [144]. Polymeric NPs are particularly useful for the preparation of hydrophobic cancer medications that have shown improved bioavailability [145].

Synthesis of polymeric hybrid NPs can generally be grouped into four strategies, which are depicted in Fig. 7.14 . One way is to synthesize both polymer and inorganic components ex situ and combine them to form hybrid NPs. In this strategy polymer chains can be attached to inorganic particles by coating them or by being grafted on the surface of inorganic particles. Alternatively, polymer NPs can be combined with inorganic NPs [144]. Second strategy of polymeric hybrid NP formation is through ex situ synthesis of inorganic component (typically inorganic NPs) and polymerization of the organic component in its presence [144]. Third main synthesis strategy is to fabricate the polymer or the polymer components ex situ and then in situ synthesize inorganic component through precipitation or crystallization. Last main strategy is to synthesize both the polymer and the inorganic components simultaneously in situ, during hybrid NP formation [144].

Some of the common choices of biodegradable polymers for polymeric hybrid NPs are poly(D,L-lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA), polycaprolactone (PCL), dextran, or albumin due to their biocompatibility, biodegradability, nontoxicity, and previous use in approved products [146,147]. Biodegradable polymeric NPs derived from PLGA containing high loadings of a molecular cargo have been fabricated using arrested precipitation and water-in-oil-in-oil (W/O/O) double emulsion techniques [148,149].

As part of the inorganic component of polymeric hybrid NPs, imaging contrast agents have attracted wide attention in literature. For example, Gd-loaded polymeric NPs for T1-weighted MRI have been used for detection of atherosclerosis applications [150]. High amounts of an FDA-approved positive MR contrast agent, diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid gadolinium (III), have been loaded using W/O/O double emulsion solvent evaporation method in to PLGA NPs [19]. Biodegradable polymer NPs loaded with fluorescent cancer drug DOX and coated with breast-cancer specific peptides have been used for detection of tumor cites in mice using fluorescent and ultrasound imaging techniques [151]. Feng and co-workers developed a PLGA NP system containing magnetic nanocrystals for MRI [152]. Due to the close proximity of the magnetic NPs, magnetic properties of these hybrids were significantly better relative to individual magnetic nanocrystals, which improved the MR contrast effect [8]. Improved MR contrast was confirmed when multiple magnetic nanocrystals were placed in close proximity to liver from mice injected with the hybrids. Liu et al. showed a possibility of magnetically guided drug delivery and other biomagnetic applications using these PLGA NPs [153].

Combining functional nanocrystals (fluorescent QDs of MRI-visible magnetic NPs) and therapeutic agents have been found to be compatible with polymeric NPs [154–157]. For example, several works report synthesis of biodegradable and surfactant-free NPs loaded with hydrophobic drugs and with hydrophobic superparamagnetic nanocrystals or QDs using arrested precipitation method [158] or using oil-in-water emulsion and subsequent solvent evaporation techniques [159]. Polymeric NPs made of magnetic nanocrystals core which together with the anticancer drug DOX have been encapsulated into the thermoresponsive triblock copolymer have shown theranostic applications too [160]. Controlled drug release has been successfully shown in response to the physiological temperature caused by deswelling of the thermoresponsive block polymer [160].

LPHNs are gaining increasing attention in clinical trials due to their attractive properties for drug delivery [23,161]. The LPHNs represent theoretically superior delivery system by combining biomimetic characteristics of lipids and mechanical properties of polymer core [161]. The LPHNs are largely used for delivery platforms and comprise three components as illustrated in Fig. 7.1 [23]. The first component is a polymer core in which the therapeutic substances can be entrapped. The second is an inner lipid layer enveloping the polymer core, which confers biocompatibility to the polymer core. Finally, the third component of LPHNs is an outer lipid PEG layer, which functions as a stealth coating that prolongs in vivo circulation time of the LPHNs, as well as provides steric stabilization [23,161–164].

In comparison with other types of nanocarriers, LPHNs are unique due to several advantages such as range of lipids, biocompatible polymers, various polymer–lipid combinations, and a superior ability to co-encapsulate different therapeutic and imaging agents [162,165]. However, LPHNs are particularly widely used as an alternative approach in anticancer therapy representing a valuable therapeutics delivery system that protects drugs in the bloodstream, increases their biodistribution, and limits their adverse effects by achieving target specificity [162,165,166]. Their ability to encapsulate therapeutics and release them in a controlled or sequential manner ensures the efficacy of combinatorial cancer therapy [166,167]. Various bioactive molecules such as drugs, genes, proteins, peptides, and targeting ligands can be entrapped, adsorbed, or attached in the hybrid system [161]. For example, co-delivery of anticancer drugs and genes can lead to promising synergistic anticancer effects [168]. Huang et al. synthesized aptamer-coated LPHNs for the co-delivery of paclitaxel and DOX to cancer cells with high specificity and efficiency [169].

In another study, tripeptide RGD-decorated lipid polymeric hybrid NPs were used for the co-delivery of DOX and sorafenib based on the ability of cells to internalize RGD-containing macromolecules, which significantly enhanced antitumor efficiency in a mouse xenograft model of hepatocellular carcinoma [168]. Yang et al. reported on producing cyclic RGD-modified LPHNs for the targeted delivery of 10-hydroxycamptothecin to human breast cancer cells [170]. Additionally, Wang et al. fabricated targeted tumor-penetrating NPs for the simultaneous delivery of a photosensitizer indocyanine green and hypoxia-activated prodrug tirapazamine for PDT and hypoxia-activated chemotherapy applications [171]. In a recent study, Zhang et al. developed core-shell LPHNs for the combined genetic and chemical therapy of childhood head and neck cancers [172].

Recent advances in LPHN-based cancer treatment also include the development of smart LPHNs that respond to external or internal stimuli to enhance the therapeutic efficacy and site specificity [170]. For example, Clawson et al. designed a novel LPHN with pH-triggered PEG shedding. This is especially useful in anticancer drug delivery because it exploits the slightly acidic extracellular space of tumors (pH ~6.5) [164]. Upon arrival at the tumor site, a correctly adjusted pH-sensitive particle would shed its PEG coating and consequently fuse with the cell membrane and be internalized [164,173]. Apart from drug delivery application, recent work on the use of LPHNs has been also expanded to transportation of genetic materials [174], vaccines [175], and diagnostic imaging agents [26].

Gold-based hybrid NPs comprise components that enable diagnostic and therapeutic functions and take an advantage of unique physical properties of gold NPs that differ from those of both bulk gold and other NPs. One of the most important optical properties of gold NPs originates from plasmons, which are collective oscillations of highly mobile electrons in the metal at optical frequencies [19]. Surface plasmons that are found on gold NP surfaces allow efficient coupling of external optical fields into photoemissive, thermal, or chemical effects [19]. Therefore, gold NPs are effective at converting the light into heat, and enhancing the local electromagnetic fields [176]. They are also efficient at scattering the light, which make them good contrast agents for imaging applications. Additionally, they can be functionalized with various molecules, ensuring preferential accumulation in their biological targets [177]. Gold-based NPs have been widely used for cancer diagnostics and therapy [178] and are suitable for a wide range of biological imaging such as computed tomography (CT) [179–183], optical coherence tomography [184–187], two-photon-induced photoluminescence [188–190], and photoacoustic imaging [191,192].

Gold-based hybrid NPs with the components that enable diagnostic and therapeutic functions are most often based on one or several plasmonic particles that by themselves can consist of several composites (silica/gold, gold/silver, etc.). These particles are surrounded by a biopolymeric, silica, or another dielectric shell/coating [176]. The outer coating can be doped with various reporter molecules (fluorescent, near infrared (NIR), Raman, PD, etc.) and/or molecules of the substances being delivered (drugs, peptides, siRNA, etc.) [193]. Such hybrid NPs also include one important type of composite of gold and magnetic NPs, which allows taking an advantage of plasmonic and magnetic functionalities for theranostic applications [193].

Synthesis of gold NPs is well established, and they can be fabricated in various shapes such as sphere, cube, rod, cage, and wire. Control of the shape influences greatly the physical properties of the products and in turn affects their role as imaging probes [16]. For example, surface plasmonic absorption of gold NPs can change from red-shift to NIR region of spectrum as the gold nanostructure shape changes from spherical to rode-like [16]. Therefore, it is important to consider the structure of gold-based hybrid NPs because their functionality can vary greatly, and their applicability to theranostics should be chosen accordingly.

Research on gold-based hybrid NPs for theranostic applications has seen a vast growth in recent years. Gold-based hybrid NPs are composites of one or more other materials, examples of which are briefly discussed below.

Most widely used types of gold-based hybrid NPs in biomedicine are based on composites of gold with polymers or with NPs of other metals and semiconductors [194–196]. Among them, the earliest example of such hybrid NPs is a composite of gold NPs with polymeric NPs made of poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) dendrimer [197]. Due to cellular penetration, these NPs increased the contrast in optical imaging and X-ray CT [198] and the efficacy of tumor radiotherapy with PAMAM-GNP-Au [199]. Similarly, Li et al. showed PT therapy (PTT) and chemotherapy treatment of HeLa cells and colon carcinoma tumors in mouse by conjugating DOX cancer drug to the dendrimer layer of PEG-PAMAM to gold nanorods (GNR) [200].

However, gold/polymer hybrid NPs are particularly appealing for cancer theranostic applications, as they can be used as various theranostic modalities such as PTT, PDT, chemotherapy, and imaging probes [201]. They are particularly interesting as an agent for multimodal imaging and offer synergetic combination of different therapy methods summarized in Fig. 7.15
[201]. For example, anticancer drugs can be entrapped inside surfactant layers surrounding AuNP or encapsulated in polymer brushes on AuNP surface or attached to AuNPs. Since hybrid gold/polymer NPs have PT properties, such cancer drug loaded NPs can offer both chemotherapy and PTT at the same time when the drug is released. The drug release itself can be altered by NIR irradiation [202–206], because gold NPs can scatter incident light at specific wavelength including the wavelength at NIR light region [207,208]. NIR light is unique due to its lower absorbance and scattering properties, allowing higher heat generation at its incidence. Thus drug release can be accelerated through polymer degradation from heat or drug diffusion. For example, when DOX was loaded in PEG-b-PCL–coated GNR, the DOX release was accelerated from 7% to 68.3% by NIR light irradiation and a NIR-triggered “on-off” release mode was also demonstrated [202].

Gold-based hybrid NPs allow not only combining biological imaging and PTT as described earlier, but also they can be successfully used for MRI by adding ferromagnetic material into them. The combination of the magnetic properties of a ferromagnetic material and the optical property of gold make such hybrid NPs to be widely used for theranostic applications. Both GNPs with a magnetic shell and NPs with a magnetic core and a gold shell have been reported [209–211].

Another type of reported gold hybrid NPs is based on composite of gold-based core and a mesoporous silica shell doped with photodynamic (PD) dyes. Such NPs are suitable for PD therapy, fluorescence microscopy, and surface-enhanced resonance spectroscopy [39,212]. For example, Khlebtsov et al. used such gold nanostructures coated with SiO2 for combined PD and PT inactivation of antibiotic-resistant S. aureus (Fig. 7.16 ), for enhanced killing of HeLa cancer cells [213] and for treatment of large-volume tumor induced rats [214,215]. Hyeon et al. designed multifunctional magnetic gold hybrid NPs consisting of a silica nanosphere core surrounded by a gold nanoshell for PTT, with magnetic NPs for MRI contrast enhancement [216].

In general, gold NPs have a long history of use in research and science, and their combination with other types of NPs allow to discover more and more remarkable properties of Graphene-based hybrid nanoparticles (GHNPs).

Graphene is a flat monolayer of carbon atoms tightly packed into a two-dimensional (2D) honeycomb lattice [217]. It can be formed into different structures, particularly rolled into 1D carbon nanotube structure (CNTs). CNTs are hydrophobic nanostructures consisting of a single graphite sheet (SWNTs) or of array of SWNTs concentrically nested like rings of a tree trunk called multiwalled nanotubes (MWNTs) shown in Fig. 7.17 . CNTs are materials with diameters of a few nanometers with good mechanical and optical properties [218,219]. CNTs are used in variety of fields, including Raman and photoacoustic imaging, drug delivery [16], and photo-ablation therapy [220–222].

Combination of the beneficial characteristics of graphene/CNTs and NPs has led to novel hybrid NPs. Graphene-based hybrid NPs can be prepared by various ways of synthesis. Synthesis method can be divided to two main classes based on the structural morphologies of the final hybrids [226]. First way of synthesis is to make GHNPs by attaching NPs on graphene or its derivatives. Second way of synthesis is to wrap NPs with graphene or its derivatives. Preference for one or another method is given depending on the size of NPs. If NP size is less than 100nm, then the NPs are small enough to be easily attached onto the graphene sheet. However, if NPs are bigger than 100nm and comparable with graphene, then the 2D sheet of graphene can be used to wrap around them [226].

If NPs attached on the surface of graphene, their deposition on graphene sheets can be performed via methods called thermal evaporation [227], pulse laser deposition [228], or sputtering [229]. Additionally, GHNPs can be synthesized ex situ by mixing the presynthesized NPs with graphene, which offers control over the size, shape, and functionality of the particles [226]. For larger NPs, their integration with graphene materials is usually carried out by ex situ wrapping graphene around them [230–233].

GHNPs formed by integrating graphene with different types of NPs (QDs, metallic NPs, and mesoporous silica NPs) have many biological applications, including in cancer treatment and chemotherapy. Because graphene is a biocompatible material, it is widely used in biological applications such as light-induced therapeutics [234], tissue engineering, and drug delivery [235]. Carbon nanotubes administered into mice have been reported to be nontoxic and excretable via either renal or biliary pathways and were found biocompatible too [236,237]. Consequently, integrating this biocompatible material with already widely used types of NPs made of noble metals [178], iron oxide [238–240], and mesoporous silica [241,242] allows many research groups to study GHNPs for their uses in bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, etc. Combination of these two kinds of nanomaterials generates synergy effects particularly applicable to cancer treatment and theranostics.

Applications of GHNPs in PTT and PDT have been studied using various graphene-based hybrid NPs that possess large absorption coefficients in the NIR region, which can be achieved by having a component of GNR [243], gold nanocubes [244], QDs [245] in the hybrid components. A study with reduced GO/Au hybrid NPs successfully showed that PT energy conversion significantly increased in comparison to its respective single components [246]. By controlling the size and shape of gold NPs, the PT efficiency of the final hybrid could be greatly enhanced, which is useful for theranostic applications [246]. In another study, in vitro cancer therapy applications of GHNPs have been shown using HeLa cells via upconversion NPs decorated with PEGylated GO sheets. Similarly, in vivo dual model of bioimaging and PT tumor destruction was successfully shown using iron oxide/gold co-decorated GO (GO-IONP-Au-PEG) [247]. The authors decorated GO by both IONPs and gold, forming multifunctional magnetic and plasmonic GO-IONP-Au NPs exhibiting strong superparamagnetic properties, and significantly enhanced optical absorbance in NIR region. Coating these NPs with PEG allowed obtaining a high stability in physiological environments and negligible in vitro toxicity [247]. The work showed that with more components loaded on graphene sheets synergy properties of the hybrid NPs increase (Fig. 7.18 ). Shi et al. reported luminescent multiwalled carbon nanotubes labeled with QDs to make use of NIR luminescence [248]. They incorporated anticancer drug into multiwalled carbon nanotubes that have a larger inner volume than single-walled carbon nanotubes. Incorporating them with QDs allowed resulting nanotubes to display strong emission in the visible spectrum and to be tracked by anatomical location in a mouse.

Photoacoustic and PT imaging of carbon nanotube hybrid NPs also have been investigated. For example, Kim and co-workers produced the gold-coated single-walled carbon nanotubes, which displayed strong plasmonic resonances in the NIR spectrum [249]. By decorating these structures with biomolecules specific to endothelial receptors, the gold-nanotube hybrids were able to target lymphatic vessels in a live animal, and susceptible to real-time monitoring by integrated photo-acoustic/PT technique. In another work, carbon nanotubes were decorated with gold nanocrystals and aptamers for targeted surface plasmon resonance imaging and photo-thermal therapy of cancer cells [250].

Several types of magnetic hybrid carbon nanotubes have been reported for magnetic actuation and MRI [251–253]. Superparamagnetic IONPs have been widely used for hybrid NP combinations as a MRI contrast agent, and aggregates formed from them have led to enhancement of performance [254–256]. A hybrid of iron oxide/GO with aminodextran-coated IONPs showed better MRI sensitivity, as IONPs were able to aggregate better due to GO [257]. One study used asymmetric single-walled carbon nanotube/IONP complexes as multimodal biomedical imaging agents [258]. The authors showed that the produced hybrid NPs can internalize into macrophage cells, and both MR and NIR fluorescence could be used for imaging them. The particles were also used for MR-guided PT treatment of tumors in a mouse xenograft model due to the intrinsic PT properties of NPs [259]. Miyawaki et al. demonstrated a feasibility of in vivo MR imaging using single-walled carbon nanotubes of 2–5nm diameters labeled with magnetite nanocrystals [260]. In vivo toxicity studies showed that synthesized hybrid NPs were biocompatible at doses up to 8mg/kg body weight. Additionally, both MRI-active NPs and QDs combined carbon nanotube hybrids have been reported, which allowed multimodal cellular imaging [261,262].

Graphene/carbon nanotube–based hybrid NPs are applicable in vast number of biomedical applications and cancer treatment. However, the size of graphene and the toxicity of metal/metal oxide NPs remain a challenging aspect for practical application of carbon nanotube-based hybrid nanoparticles (CNHNPs) in vivo [226]. Because the size of NPs is a deciding factor in permeability and retention effect in in vivo experiments, oversize graphene particles need extra step of size reduction, which can be done by centrifugation or vacuum membrane filtration [263]. Additionally, long-term monitoring of toxicity effects of NPs used largely with graphene or graphene composites has not been fully investigated and remains an ongoing issue for graphene-based hybrid NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hybrid NPs for PDT and molecular imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The unique features of hybrid NPs can be effectively harnessed for PDT. PDT is based on energy transfer leading to the formation of cytotoxic singlet oxygen and ROS [264,265]. Despite the progress in research, the exact mechanism of action of PDT still remains unclear. The general principle of the action involves the activation of photosensitizer by light illumination followed by generation of the ROS such as highly active singlet oxygen. The Fig. 7.19 depicts the scheme of energy transfer and generating of singlet oxygen (1O2) during PDT reaction [265]. The formed ROS, including 1O2, react with components of the cell such as cytoplasmic membrane or intracellular structure, leading to apoptosis induction, and consequently to cell death [266]. It has been also shown that PDT induces DNA breaks [267] and distortion of membrane transport systems [268].

The key element of PD reaction and energy transfer is a photosensitizer, which can be activated by light with specific wavelength [269]. There is a range of photosensitizers of different origins and structures. One of the most well-known photosensitizer is hematoporphyrin, which has been intensively studied and exploited since 1960s. Apart from hematoporphyrin and other porphyrins, a new range of potential therapeutic photosensitizers has emerged last decades. It includes Tin etiopurpurin, phthalocyanines (AIPcSn), lutetium texaphyrin, and chlorine-e6 [265].
