Most of them possess antitumor specificity that is based on an ability to accumulate in tumor tissues and induce PD reaction locally. It must be noted that PDT has also a great potential for the treatment of a number of noncancer diseases such as psoriasis, macular degeneration of the retina, atherosclerotic plaque and restenosis, treatment of some autoimmune disorders, and suppressing infections, and viral activity [270].

In spite of high therapeutic potential of PDT, its widespread application has been hampered due to the limitations related to intrinsic chemical and physical properties of photosensitizers. First of all, the main problem for effective administration of PDT remains a low water solubility of photosensitizers resulting in insufficient cellular uptake [271]. To overcome those limitations, a number of nanomaterials have been suggested to improve distribution and bioavailability of photosensitizers. This includes incorporation of photosensitizers molecules to nanocarriers made of organic (polymers, micelles, liposomes, and carbon) and nonorganic (gold, silver, silica, and QDs) components. The nanoplatforms for PDT must meet specific requirements, including minimal toxicity, high drug encapsulation capacity, good biodegradability, and bioavailability, and they should not alter porphyrin’s properties [272].

Organic platforms offer the possibility of safe and effective drug delivery for porphyrin-based therapeutics. Most extensively studied organic carriers for delivery of photosensitizers are liposomes. Hydrophobic photosensitizers can be encapsulated into lipid shell of liposomes providing prolonged time of circulation in the body and accumulation in tumor site [19]. The liposomes showed a proven efficiency for producing a high local drug concentration at desired location and safe removal from the organism after destruction. Choi et al. demonstrated a feasibility to generate intracellular protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) by administration of 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) incorporated into liposome carriers, where 5-ALA acted as prodrug for PpIX [273]. Compared to ALA itself, introducing liposome-ALA particles to culture of human cholangiocarcinoma HuCC-T1 cells led to an increase of phototoxic effect, and consequently, to an augmentation of cancer cells death.

Poor solubility of photosensitizers remains a main obstacle for their effective implementation into clinical practice. Sadzuka et al. used method of liposomalization to improve biodistribution of availability of photosensitizer Zn-complexed coproporphyrin I (ZnCPI) [274]. The researchers showed that encapsulation of ZnCPI into liposomes resulted in higher plasma concentration of the drug in mice with Ehrlich ascites carcinoma compered to sham group. In another work conducted by Igarashi et al. photosensitizer photofrin was entrapped in multilamellar liposomes [275] resulting in high tumor accumulation. Earlier, Jiang and coworkers reported on effective employing liposome-encapsulated photofrin for PDT of U87 human glioma in mice [276]. Alternative application of porphyrin for cancer treatment is antiangiogenic PDT, where lipid nanoplatforms can assist to achieve longer circulation time and higher local concentration. Oku and Ishii demonstrated an effectiveness of liposome carriers with an active-targeting probe (Ala-Pro-Arg-Pro-Gly) for delivery a photosensitizer benzoporphyrin derivative monoacid ring A on mice model [277].

It must be noted that there are few liposomal photosensitizers that have been already approved for clinical applications: Visudyne (Novartis Ophthalmics, Switzerland and QLT, Canada) used for treatment age-related macular degeneration and pathological myopia, and Foslip for cancer treatment (Sanofi-Aventis, France) [278–280].

Apart from therapeutic applications, porphyrins can be effectively utilized for NIR fluorescence imaging due to the presence of their emission wavelengths in the NIR spectrum and their ability to form J aggregates (p-p stacking) [281]. Hayashi et al. synthesized silica–porphyrin hybrid nanotubes using π–π stacking, electrostatic interaction, and sol-gel reaction for in vivo cell tracking by for NIR fluorescence imaging [77]. Carter et al. showed that porphyrin–phospholipid liposomes can be exploited for controlled release of encapsulated antibiotic (gentamicin) and fluorophores following intratumoral injection [282]. The researchers used devinyl hexyloxyethy-pyropheophorbide (HPPH)-based monomers to form bilayers with advanced properties. They employed molecular dynamic simulations to prove that the hexyl ether moiety is able of providing the space for drug filling between the two bilayer leaflets compared with the pyro–lipid bilayer. It found out that HPPH-lipid platform was effective in release of uploaded substances (calcein and DOX) upon laser NIR irradiation. The drug release and NIR-induced permeabilization of porphyrin–phospholipid liposomes were not accompanied by heating effect. In addition, Carter et al. demonstrated an ability of porphyrin–phospholipid liposomes to be utilized as delivery vehicles for gentamicin (in vitro conditions). The release of loaded gentamicin was occurred upon laser exposure resulting in killing pathogenic bacteria.

The data of recent reports indicate that liposomes can be coupled with nonorganic moieties such as metallic and magnetic NPs. Such a combination would provide an opportunity to develop a “theranostic” platform for effective diagnostics and therapy of different disorders, including cancer. It encompasses an encapsulation of metallic and magnetic particles into liposome-based nanocarriers for MRI, magnetic targeting, and magnetic hyperthermia [283]. Moreover, it was demonstrated that hyperthermia and PDT can be combined by utilizing hybrid magnetic liposomes. In the study carried out by Bolfarini et al. magnetic liposomes loaded with a photosensitizer (zinc phthalocyanine complexed with cucurbituril) were developed and evaluated for PD and hyperthermia effects on melanoma cells (B16-F10) [284]. The strongest cytotoxic effect was observed in the group treated by combination of PD reaction and heat shock, compared to the groups exposed to these modalities separately. Di Corato et al. synthesized ultramagnetic NPs entrapped into the liposome core [283] where a photosensitizer (m-THPC, Foscan) was introduced into the lipid bilayer (Fig. 7.20 ). Such a strategy provides an opportunity to attack tumor by using synergetic action of hyperthermia (induced by alternating magnetic field) and ROS (formed as a result of PD reaction). These ultramagnetic liposomes were tested in vitro (human adenocarcinoma SKOV-3 cells) and in vivo (mice with epidermoid carcinoma), where they demonstrated a high efficacy in tumor cell uptake, cell killing effect, and tumor regression in vivo.

Aside from employing liposome-based carriers to overcome poor solubility and toxicity of photosensitizers, researchers also proposed to utilize other organic and nonorganic nanoplatforms. First of all, it concerns the use of polymeric NPs of various origins. The polymer-based delivery systems provide a high drug loading capacity, safe degradability, good biocompatibility, and an ability to design different compositions [272].

Konan and co-workers synthesized polymeric NPs made of poly-D,L-lactide-co-glycolide (PLGA) and poly-D,L-lactide with incorporated photosensitizer (meso-tetra(hydroxyphenyl)porphyrin (p-THPP)) using the emulsification–diffusion technique [285]. The fabricated p-THPP-loaded NPs demonstrated a high photo-cytotoxic efficiency on EMT-6 mammary tumor cells in comparison with the incorporated photosensitizer. It conforms to the study conducted by Vargas et al. where PD activity of porphyrin (THPP) was improved by encapsulation it into polymeric biodegradable PLGA NPs [286]. The results of in vivo study (on chick embryos) indicated that such a combination led to improving pharmacokinetic properties of photosensitizer (THPP) and an enhancement of vascular effects induced by PDT.

The similar photosensitizer (meso-tetrakis (4-sulphonatophenyl) porphyrin (TPPS)) has been entrapped into polymethylmethacrylate NPs for ultrasound-assisted PDT (i.e., “sonodynamic” therapy) [287]. These hybrid NPs have been tested on rat breast cancer model (rat mammary adenocarcinoma cell line, Mat B III). The results showed that sonodynamic therapy by means of TPPS-polymeric NPs is able of increasing biodistribution, tumor accumulation, and anticancer activity that has been proved by histological and biochemical examinations. In another study dedicated to sonodynamic therapy, TPPS-polymeric NPs also demonstrated cytotoxic effect on human neuroblastoma cell line SH-SY5Y [288].

It was demonstrated that liposome and polymer platforms can be successfully coupled for anticancer PDT. John et al. fabricated hybrid lipopolymeric NPs to deliver photosensitizer chlorin e6 (Ce6) (Fig. 7.21 ) [289]. Lipo-polymers consisted of soybean lecithin derived phosphatidylcholine, phosphatidylethanolamine-poly(l-histidine)40, and FA conjugated phosphatidylethanolamine-poly(N-isopropylacrylamide)40. The researchers showed that Ce6-encapsulation into lipopolymeric nanoplatform led to more effective cellular uptake, improved intracellular distribution, and enhanced phototoxicity against cancer cells compared to noncapsulated Ce6 at different temperatures and pH conditions.

Another example of hybrid nanoplatform for cancer treatment is polycation–liposomal NPs. Takeuchi and coworkers proposed to employ such NPs for tumor suppression by means of PDT [290]. In the study researchers used partially acetylated polyethylenimine to coat the liposomal membrane by van der Waals and hydrophobic interactions with the hydrophobic region of the liposome. The polycation–liposomes with entrapped photosensitizer benzoporphyrin derivative monoacid ring A were administrated to mice with Meth-A sarcoma followed by exposure to laser irradiation. The researchers reported on neovascular destruction of tumor tissue and suppression of tumor growth accompanied by induction of apoptosis (“programmed cell death”).

Recently the application of organic nanoplatforms for PDT has been extended to carbon nanomaterials, in which biological and chemical properties can be improved by conjugating with polymers or antibodies [271]. One of most exploited carbon-based moieties is carbon nanotubes. In range of studies, it was demonstrated that carbon nanotubes is a potential modality for delivery of photosensitizer to cancer cells. Huang et al. reported on fabrication of polyamidoamine dendrimer modified multiwalled carbon nanotubes for delivery a photosensitizer 5-ALA to tumor MGC-803 cells [291]. The data of the study showed a successful generation of ROS upon laser exposure resulted in the damage of cancer cells.

One of ideal candidates for PDT is metallophthalocyanines that can absorb the light in the red diapason of electromagnetic spectrum. To improve bioavailability and tumor targeting properties of metallophthalocyanine, zinc mono carboxy phenoxy-phthalocyanine was conjugated to spermine (ZnMCPPc-spermine), and then adsorbed onto single-walled carbon nanotubes [292]. Such a combination was most effective in inducing PD reaction and cell killing effect for MCF-7 cancer cells compared to ZnMCPPc-spermine, which was not conjugated with single-walled carbon nanotubes. Apart from this study, there is a range of reports on successful incorporation of photosensitizers onto single-walled carbon nanotubes for PDT and PTT [293–298].

Besides carbon nanotubes, GO can be also utilized for delivery the photosensitizer molecules to the treatment site [293–296,298,299]. Wei et al. synthesized a hybrid nanoplatform comprising nano-GO modified with the integrin αvβ3 mAb for selective tumor targeting [300]. The surface of hybrid NPs was covered by second-generation photosensitizer, pyropheophorbide-a, which has been conjugated with polyethylene glycol. The results showed that such combination was capable to target the αvβ3-positive tumor cells with surface ligand and receptor recognition followed by transfer to the mitochondria and accumulation. The exposure to laser led to activation of pyropheophorbide-a, induction of PD reaction, and generation ROS. As a result, decrease of cell viability and apoptosis induction was observed. Interestingly, human breast cancer cells line (MCF-7) was found to be more resistant to the treatment with hybrid NPs and laser than adherent human glioblastoma (U87-MG) cell line. This might be caused by high molecular size of NPs, which hampered cellular uptake. A similar strategy to target αvβ3 receptors on cancer cells by using hybrid GO-based NPs was employed by Jang et al. [301]. The researchers fabricated a dual ligand targeting nano-GO by noncovalent interaction with folate and Cyclic Arg-Gly-Asp functionalized pluronic. The results of in vitro study showed increased cellular uptake of dual ligand modified NPs compared to single ligand modified NPs. These data were confirmed by results of in vivo study, where high tumor accumulation and tumor destruction upon laser irradiation were observed. In another study, GO was used to improve bioavailability of photosensitizer Hypocrellin A and anticancer drug 7-ethyl-10-hydroxycamptothecin (SN-38) [302]. For this purpose, the drugs were co-loaded to the surface of GO NPs using a simple noncovalent method. The obtained results indicated that the Hypocrellin A/SN-38/GO combination had higher anticancer activity compared to Hypocrellin A/GO NPs and SN-38/GO NPs.

Among all inorganic nanoplatforms for PDT, gold NPs gained the main attention due to their high biocompatibility and surface functionality [271,303]. The photosensitizer molecules can be either attached to the surface of gold NPs or be incorporated into the core. Both methods have own advantages and drawbacks [304].

A feasibility of conjugating phthalocyanine-based photosensitizers to the surface of gold NPs for PDT was demonstrated in the range of studies [305–309]. Phthalocyanines belong to the last generation of promising photosensitizers [310]. Comparing to classic photosensitizers such as porphyrin, phthalocyanines possess better physical and chemical properties. First of all, phthalocyanine derivatives have absorption peak in NIR region of the visible spectra making possible for the light to penetrate the tissues. Moeno et al. reported on improved photophysical behavior of zinc phthalocyanine peripherally tetra substituted with 1,6-hexanedithiol when it was conjugated to gold NPs (Fig. 7.22 ) [311]. In another work, Nombone et al. synthesized and assesses phototoxic properties of Zn-phthalocyanine ([2,9,17,23-tetrakis-(1,6-exanedithiol)phthalocyaninato]zinc(II)) conjugated with gold NPs and liposomes [306]. Camerin et al. used polyethylene glycolated gold NP conjugates to deliver of Zn(ii)-phthalocyanine for PDT of amelanotic melanoma [312].

To improve biocompatibility and stability of gold nanocomposites for PDT, researchers proposed to coat particles with different materials such as heparin [313], lipids [314,315], polymers [316,317], and silica [318,319]. For example, Vijayaraghavan et al. combined gene anticancer therapy and lipid-coated gold NPs (“nanoechinus”) for the PD treatment of deep tissue buried tumors [314]. Polyanionic siRNAs (SOD1 gene) were complexed with the cationic lipid-coated Au NEs via electrostatic interactions. The cell culture was exposed to the laser with three different wavelengths (808, 915, and 1064nm). The data demonstrated a linear correlation between cytotoxicity and range of wavelength, where the most cytotoxic effect was found for the groups irradiated by 1064nm laser in the presence hybrid siRNA-lipid coated gold NPs as a result of PDT and gene silencing.

Another alternative nanoplatform for drug delivery and PDT is silica NPs. This platform allows tailoring and modification the surface of NPs with various groups to improve specific binding and decrease aggregation [320,321]. Silica-based NPs provide an opportunity to load drugs and photosensitizers due their high loading capacity [271].

One of the first reports on successful incorporation of photosensitizer into silica-based nanoplatform has been made by Yan and Kopelman [322]. According to their report, meta-tetra (hydroxyphenyl)-chlorin (Foscam) was successfully embedded into amine-functionalized silica NPs leading to production of singlet oxygen, which plays a main role in PD reactions. Encouraged by these findings, researchers endeavored to optimize and fabricate silica-based NPs coupled with various photosensitizers. For example, silicalites and mesoporous silica NPs with incorporated porphyrin-based photosensitizer were synthesized for PDT by Hocine et al. [76]. The surface of NPs was functionalized with mannose to improve cellular uptake that was confirmed by confocal microscopy analysis. The phototoxicity was observed upon light irradiation on culture of human breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231). In another study, mesoporous silica NPs for one-photon-excited PDT combined with drug delivery and carbohydrate (mannose/galactose) cell targeting were fabricated (Fig. 7.23 ) [75]. It was demonstrated that the combination of anticancer drug (camptothecin) and mesoporous silica NPs functionalized with mannose on the surface was effective in inducing death of retinoblastoma cancer cells.

A recent trend in development of silica-based platforms for PDT is ORMOSIL NPs [115,323–325]. This platform offers a feasibility to load drugs either to a hydrophobic shell or to a hydrophilic core preventing them from untimely degradation and removal from the organism [326]. Kumar and coworkers developed spherical and oval-shaped NPs with hydroxyl and amine groups that were synthesized in nanomicelles formed by Tween 80 and butanol served as nanoreactors for entrapping fluorescent dye molecules [324]. Data of fluorescent microscopy revealed that NPs with -OH functionality were taken up by the cells faster than those having surface -NH2 groups. The researchers reported on size-dependent NP toxicity indicating that particles less than 20nm possess higher toxicity compared to those particles of larger size but less than 100nm diameter. Shirshahi et al. used normal micelle microemulsion method for fabrication of ORMOSIL NPs conjugated to herceptin to target overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 on breast cancer cell line SKBR3 (Fig. 7.24 ) [327]. Biocompatibility and toxicity of organically modified silica is a big issue, and this was a topic of a range of published works [328–331]. Using Drosophila as a biological model, Barandeh et al. showed that ORMOSIL are biocompatible and not toxic to whole organism, and they possess a potential for the development of long-term applications [332].

Amid all nanomaterials, the QDs-based nanoplatforms possess the most unique and effective optical properties making them an ideal candidate for PDT [333–337]. These include a large absorption spectra, symmetric emission bands, high photostability, and biocompatibility compared to other nanoplatforms [338]. It must be noted that at the same time QDs can act as effective drug carriers or luminescent nanoprobes [339].

One of first published reports on possibility of using QDs for PDT is work of Samia et al. [340]. The researchers synthesized CdSe QDs (diameter 5nm) conjugated with silicon photosensitizer (phthalocyanine) through an alkyl amino group. It was demonstrated an ability of CdSe QDs to excite the photosensitizer at 488nm through a fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism followed by generation of singlet oxygen.

The surface of QDs can be modified through the conjugating with various organic and nonorganic materials to enhance cell targeting. Group of Li et al. demonstrated an efficacy of PDT by means of QDs conjugated with integrin antagonist arginine–glycine–aspartic acid peptides in in vivo study [341]. Earlier the same group published report on successful synthesis of arginine–glycine–aspartic acid-conjugated QDs to be employed as a marker for pancreatic carcinoma cells [342]. These cells express a high level of ανβ3 integrin receptors, which play an important role in tumor pathogenesis. It was found out that arginine–glycine–aspartic acid-conjugated QDs bound specifically to pancreatic carcinoma cells through ligand-receptor mechanism.

Another vital receptor that is overexpressed by cancer cells is folate (FA) receptor. FA is a stable molecule at a broad range of temperatures and pH values, it is completely nonimmunogenic. FA retains its ability to bind to the folate receptor after conjugation with nanocarriers of various origin [343]. It was demonstrated that the surface of QDs can be functionalized with FAs [344–347]. Geszke et al. produced water soluble manganese-doped core/shell ZnS/ZnS QDs conjugated with molecules of FAs with low cytotoxicity [348]. Yan et al. developed a photosensitizer for the fluorescence imaging and PDT of cancer cells on the basis of a self-assembled CdSe QD-thiophene-substituted aza-BODIPY conjugate coated with poly(ethylene glycol) and FA via Förster resonance energy transfer [349]. Nicholas et al. employed FA for a molecular targeting to improve the specificity of a photosensitizer CQD-protoporphyrin IX (CQD-PPIX) conjugate to folate receptor positive HeLa cells for use in two-photon-excited PDT [350]. The results of the study demonstrated a high uptake (seven times higher) of FA-CQD-PPIX conjugate compared to CQD-PPIX conjugate. The data of PDT experiments indicated 30% improved cytotoxicity for cells treated with the FA-CQD-PPIX.

Aside from classical QDs, the range of combination and encapsulation strategies has been proposed for PDT past decade. One of the recent trends is utilizing graphene QDs. Ge and coworkers developed water-dispersible graphene QDs using a hydrothermal method with polythiophene derivatives as a carbon source [338]. A photosensitizer chlorin e6 (Ce6) was incorporated into planar structure of graphene QD or GO via a reduction cleavable disulfide linker [351]. This hybrid nanoplatform demonstrated sufficient fluorescence quenching and slight phototoxicity, even under light exposure. Compared with GO system with larger size formed hybrid graphene, QD-based NPs exhibited improved tumor accumulation through an enhanced permeation and retention effects. Thakur et al. reported on the synthesis of highly crystalline graphene QDs with superior physicochemical and NIR-responsive properties using simple waste, withered leaves of Ficus racemosa (Indian fig tree) as a carbon source. [334]. The data of cell cycle analysis and biocompatibility studies indicated that graphene QDs were biocompatible. Concentration-dependent PT response and formation of ROS were observed as a result of PD reaction.

Other strategies that must be also mentioned are related to improvement design and biofunctionality of QDs for PDT and encompass the encapsulation of QDs into silica [352,353], coating of QDs with polymers [354–357], development of hybrid QD-gold NPs [358–360], conjugating QDs with luciferase [337,361], and fabrication graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) QDs [362,363].

Each imaging technique, including positron emission tomography (PET), single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT), X-ray, CT, and MRI, has advantages and limitations. Therefore, the hybridization of imaging platform using nanosystems can help to overcome the intrinsic problems leading to effective multimodal imaging and precise diagnostics [364].

The most used imaging methods rely on administration of specific contrast agents. It has been demonstrated that nanomaterials can be effectively utilized for molecular and tissue visualization and tracking. For example, superparamagnetic IONPs have been proven to serve as effective contrast agents for MRI [365,366]. Another big advantage of using nanosystems for imaging is their ability to carry large payloads of contrast agents, including radioactive. Apart from carrying capacity, such an approach provides a protection of radioactive agents from recognition by immune system and undesirable uptake by reticuloendothelial system thus increasing their half-life and accumulation in targeted site [367]. In this regard, the “tanker” type (Type II) of hybrid nanoplatform [19] can be indispensable for transportation of radioactive compounds thus preventing them from clearance by phagocytes and oxidization (Fig. 7.25 ).

The concept of hybrid nanoplatform allows combining imaging modalities in order to enhance resolution and lower the risk for the patient. For example, in a range of studies it was showed the feasibility to fabricate dual-modality (PET/MR) imaging agents based on nanosystems. Glaus et al. developed NPs with superparamagnetic iron oxide core coated with PEGylated phospholipids, and labeled with positron-emitting 64Cu [368].

There is a range of radioisotopes employed for NPs’ labeling for assessment of biodistribution and other purposes [367]. For SPECT application the most used isotopes are Technetium-99m (99mTc) and Indium-111 (111In). It was shown that 99mTc can be directly labeled to superparamagnetic IONPs [369–371]. Li et al. developed multifunctional dendrimer-entrapped gold NPs modified with FA and labeled with 99mTc for targeted dual-mode SPECT/CT imaging of tumors (Fig. 7.26 ) [372].

Apart from metal-based platforms, it has been demonstrated that 99mTc might be conjugated/labeled to polymer NPs to improve biodistribution and SPECT/CT imaging [373]. Another radiopharmaceutic widely utilized for biodistribution studies and tissue imaging is Indium-111 (111In). It was demonstrated as a possibility to use 111In for radio-labeling NPs of various origin: superparamagnetic IONPs [374], gold NPs [375], liposomes [376], and carbon nanotubes [236].

The main labeling NPs isotopes used for PET imaging are copper-64 (64Cu), fluorine-18, bromine-76 and nitrogen-13. It was demonstrated that 64Cu labeled nanoplatforms exhibited longer blood circulation and lower uptake by immune and reticular systems. In this regard, 64Cu can be employed for labeling polymeric NPs functionalized with PEG [377,378], superparamagnetic IONPs [368], and QDs conjugated with RGD peptide [379].

In addition to the ability of labeling for effective tracking and diagnostics purposes, the radioactive hybrid nanosystems can be also utilized for therapeutic applications. In particular, such an approach has a potential to be used for targeted elimination of cancer cells by means of radioactive particles that emit α or β radiation. McLaughlin et al. developed multilayered gold-coated lanthanide phosphate NPs conjugated to mAb 201b via a lipoic acid-PEG12-COOH linker to target thrombomodulin receptors (Fig. 7.27 ) [380]. The fabricated hybrid NPs combined radio-resistant properties of lanthanide phosphate (to radioactive 225Actinium), magnetic properties of gadolinium phosphate, and provided the optimal conditions for conjugating targeting molecules (thank to gold shell).

A similar approach was employed by Rojas et al. who fabricated and characterized lanthanum phosphate NPs as carriers for 223Ra and/or 225Ra/225Ac for targeted alpha therapy [381]. Data of in vivo studies demonstrated a high retention of Ra parents and associated daughters isotopes indicating a significant improvement to chelators, which are responsible for release all α-emitting radionuclides. Authors concluded that the core and core-shell lanthanum phosphate NPs have a potential for theranostic applications to serve as carriers of radioisotopes in targeted cancer therapy, and at the same time, to facilitate diagnostic imaging.

In addition to the possibility of direct tumor destruction by means of radionuclides incorporated into nanoplatforms, hybrid NPs can be utilized as radiosensitizers for cancer radiotherapy. In particular, it was showed that gold NPs equipped with various functional groups and with modified surface have a potential for cancer treatment as radiosensitizers [382–384]. For instance, Su et al. developed hybrid nanosized cyclic Arg-Gly-Asp-conjugated gold NPs integrated with radioactive iodine-125 as tumor-targeted radiosensitizer and SPECT/CT imaging agent [385]. In this study, apoptosis induction along with the suppression of tumor growth was significantly higher in the group treated with hybrid NPs than in control groups exposed to radiotherapy only.

Apart from cancer diagnostics and therapy, it has been also demonstrated that the potential of radioactive NPs can be employed for imaging and diagnostics of other disorders, including atherosclerosis [386], ischemia [387], and for intraoperative imaging [388]. Generally speaking, the hybrid platforms offer a wide choice of combinations of radioactive agents with different delivery of nanovehicles for various diagnostic and therapeutic applications. In terms of fabrication of hybrid radioactive NPs, Lewis and Kannan stressed out that for effective functioning the radiolabeled nanoconstructs must possess high negative zeta potential and appropriate hydrodynamic size to prevent aggregation and enhance biodistribution [367].

Recent advances in nanotechnology paved the way for clinical applications of NPs. Nowadays biomedical nanosystems are becoming commercially available worldwide due to constant improvement of their biological and physical properties. The use of NPs has been extended to diagnostic and therapeutic purposes, where the main focus has been made in oncology, particularly for tumor detection and therapy as well. However, various nanomaterials have advantages and drawbacks nudging the researchers to find ways to combine two or more nanosystems in one. Hybrid systems are able to overcome intrinsic problems of classic platforms such as poor biocompatibility and high toxicity. Indeed, hybridization can assist to achieve synergetic effect originated from two or more compatible nanocomponents. The sought effect could have strictly therapeutic character or it might combine diagnostic (imaging) and curative impacts. In this regard, theranostic hybrid NPs can help to detect, monitor, and treat affected site of the body. Hybrid nanosystems provide an opportunity to increase the efficacy of treatment using standard pharmaceutical compounds, including anticancer drugs. First of all, highly chemotoxic agents can be incorporated into NPs, and therefore, be protected from early uptake by the immune system, thus improving their biodistribution and half-life. In addition, the NP surface can be modified and functionalized by ligands to enhance accumulation of the drug at affected region or organ. Such a strategy has a positive impact on pharmacokinetics, and it would minimize undesirable adverse effects.

The coupling imaging and therapeutic components is a main scenario for theranostic concept, particularly in oncology. Imaging agents (radioactive or non-radioactive) for CT, X-ray imaging, MRI, SPECT, PET, NIR fluorescence, and ultrasound imaging can be decorated with functional target molecules, and then, combined with anticancer compounds. This concept can revolutionize the classic approach for cancer management, particularly for detection and treatment of solid tumors.

Novel methods such as stimuli-responsive drug release nanosystems open an avenue for noninvasive and personalized cancer therapy. It would encompass the administration of hybrid NPs and external application of electromagnetic field, laser, or ultrasound (for example, high focused ultrasound) to detect, and then, to trigger drug release at a desired site. Such an approach provides a significant reducing dose of chemotherapeutic compound along with monitoring and evaluation of treatment (before and after administration). In addition, it was demonstrated that the surface of some types of NPs can be modified to be responsive to environmental factors, including pH level, nitric oxide, or ROS. Such a reaction can be used for triggering drug release from NP’s depot.

Another emerging area for application of hybrid NP is vaccination and immunotherapy. In a range of recent reports it has been demonstrated that hybrid nanoplatforms can play a role of carriers for effective delivery of antigens and antibodies [389–392]. Apart from strengthening immune system, hybrid NPs can also be utilized to fight infections directly [393,394]. In this context, nanomaterials can provide a shelter for antibiotics resulting in improved chemical stability and prevention of early biodegradation. Moreover, NPs surface can be potentially co-equipped with special ligands to enhance accumulation of antibiotic in the desired region of the body. Taking into account the growth of antibiotic-resistance of pathogens, the intensive research for effective antimicrobial hybrid nanosystems is highly desirable.

In spite of recent progress on theranostics, there is a range of issues related to translation of research on hybrid NPs into clinical practice. These problems are intrinsic for all types of single used nanoplatforms regardless of design and composition. It encompasses the issues with mass production, cost-efficiency, biodegradability, and toxicity. The research has to move on from in vitro work to in vivo studies thus speeding up the process of translation. In addition to technical challenges, there are also clinical and regulatory issues that must be solved prior to clinical applications.
